coredata.dc:title: “In situ” hexagonal nanostructures surface growth from boron-carbon nanodots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Gutiérrez-Sánchez, Cristina
Martínez-Periñán, Emiliano
Mateo-Martí, Eva
Lorenzo, Encarnación
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have developed a method for generating nanopatterns based on hexagonal nanostructures grown “in situ” on highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) surfaces. For this purpose, carbon nanodots doped with nitrogen and boron have been synthesized. The electrografting of the diazotized carbon nanodots has allowed the formation of hexagonal nanostructures, depending on the number of cyclic voltammetry reduction scans applied. Atomic Force Microscopy, Raman microscopy, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy have characterized the resulting nanopatterned surface. The new hexagonal-nanopatterned surface presents high capacitance, highlighting its potential application for the development of energy storage devices such as electric double-layer capacitors
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Nitrogen
Boron
Electrografting
Hexagonal nanostructures
Nanopatterns
Highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG)
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growing of hexagonal nanostructures on HOPG
Formation of hexagonal structures on HOPG
Characterization of hexagonal structures on HOPG
Hexagonal nanostructures based on B-CND as an electric double-layer capacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectrum (A), emission fluorescence spectrum (excitation wavelengths 310 nm) (B) and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (C) of B-CND. Inset of Fig. 1B shows photographs of B-CND aqueous solution at natural light (left) and under UV light (365 nm excitation) irradiation (right). TEM micrograph of B-CNDs (D). B-CND diameter histogram obtained by DLS (E). Zeta potential measurement of B-CND at different pHs (F).

Cyclic voltammograms performed at HOPG electrode of the electrografting of B-CND in 0.5 M HCl, in presence (A) and in the absence (B) of 29 mM NaNO2. (C) Cyclic voltammograms of electrografting of the CND in 0.5 M HCl in 29 mM NaNO2 at HOPG electrode. The measurements were done at 0.10 V/s scan rate and 50 CV consecutive scans.

AFM topographic images of a bare HOPG (A). HOPG after 50 voltammetry cyclic scans in the presence of B-CND treated with NaNO2 (B) and HOPG after 50 voltammetry cyclic scans in the presence of CND treated with NaNO2 (C), and their corresponding z-axis profiles show the height following the green lines in the images.

AFM topographic images of different HOPG after being exposed to 10 (A), 25 (B), 32 (C), 40 (D), 50 (E) and 60 (F) reduction cyclic scans with B-CND 1.5 × 10−2 M NaNO2 in 0.5 M HCl at 1 h and the corresponding height profiles following the green lines in the images.

AFM topographic images (A and B) and the corresponding 3D images (C and D) of HOPG after being exposed to 32 (A and C) and 50 (B and D) reductive cyclic scans with B-CND 1.5 × 10−2 M NaNO2 in 0.5 M HCl at 1 h.

AFM topographic images of a HOPG after being exposed to 50 reductive cyclic scans with B-CND 1.5 × 10−2 M NaNO2 in 0.5 M HCl at 1 h (A). (B-C) AFM topography zoom in different places. (D) Topographic profile across the line drawn in (C).

Topographic Z-axis profile vs. X-axis of HOPG after being exposed to 10 (black), 25 (red), 32 (green), 40 (blue), 50 (cyan) and 60 (yellow) reductive cyclic scans with B-CND 1.5 × 10−2 M NaNO2 in 0.5 M HCl at 1 h.
B1s and N1 XPS spectra of drop cast and electrografted B-CND on HOPG.

A) Charge/discharge curves of HOPG (black line); CND electrografted/HOPG (red line) and B-CND electrografted/HOPG (Blue line) EDLC applying a current of 4 μA/cm2 in 0.5 M H2SO4 using a two electrodes configuration. (B) Surface capacitance vs. current applied in charge/discharge experiments of exfoliated HOPG (black line); CND electrografted/HOPG (red line) and B-CND electrografted/HOPG (blue line). C) CVs of exfoliated HOPG (black line); CND electrografted/HOPG (red line) and B-CND electrografted/HOPG (blue line) electrodes at 100 mV/s scan rate in 0.5 M H2SO4 using a two electrodes configuration. (D) Scan rate response of the capacitance of exfoliated HOPG (black line); CND electrografted/HOPG (red line) and B-CND electrografted/HOPG (blue line).
(A) Diazotized B-CND electro grafting set-up. (B) Charge-discharge measurements set-up.
EDCL based on carbon nanodots comparison.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon nanodots, electrografting, nanopatterns, highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG), electric double-layer capacitors, EDLC.

head.ce:title: “In situ” hexagonal nanostructures surface growth from boron-carbon nanodots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química Analítica y Análisis Instrumental

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have developed a method for generating nanopatterns based on hexagonal nanostructures grown “in situ” on highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) surfaces. For this purpose, carbon nanodots doped with nitrogen and boron have been synthesized. The electrografting of the diazotized carbon nanodots has allowed the formation of hexagonal nanostructures, depending on the number of cyclic voltammetry reduction scans applied. Atomic Force Microscopy, Raman microscopy, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy, and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy have characterized the resulting nanopatterned surface. The new hexagonal-nanopatterned surface presents high capacitance, highlighting its potential application for the development of energy storage devices such as electric double-layer capacitors

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Open access data can be found at:

ce:section.ce:para: Nanostructuring offers significant benefits for the development of electric doubled-layer capacitor (EDLC), primarily through its ability to enhance surface area and improve electrical conductivity. By manipulating materials at the nanoscale, it is possible to create structures that maximize charge storage capabilities while reducing weight and volume. This leads to capacitors that can store more energy and discharge it more efficiently. Additionally, nanostructured materials often exhibit improved stability and longer lifespans, making them ideal for applications in renewable energy systems and electric vehicles. Overall, the integration of nanotechnology into EDLC design can result in more efficient, reliable, and compact energy storage solutions. The charge storage mechanism in EDLC comprises of the accumulation of ions at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. While charging, ions in the electrolyte move and bind to the electrodes, creating a dual layer of charges [1,2]. Taking in mind this mechanism, the increment of active surface, that is generated by surface nanostructuration, would enhance significantly the capacity in electrochemical capacitors, thanks to the increment of surface available for generation of dual layer of ions.

Nanopatterning is a key strategy that has been used for surfaces nanostructuration [3,4]. Nanopatterning surfaces is currently a beneficial strategy for different applications in nanoscience and nanotechnology, arousing great interest [5]. Different approaches have been developed targeting different functionalities, scales and substrates. These processes, which require extreme precision, have presented high versatility and can be categorized into top-down and bottom-up strategies. Among them, those based on functionalization with molecules are especially interesting [5–7].

Different approaches have been described to control the shapes and size of the patterns such as local removal of surface molecules, using a nano-shaving technique based on scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) [8,9], or by scanning probe lithography [10]. Others are based on nanolithography methods, generating V-shaped holes [11], or based on the use of masks as templates for the generation of films [12], as well as electrochemical etching-assisted methods [3,13]. An alternative approach for carbon surface functionalization using molecules is their covalent modification by electrografting, generating thin layers with great stability. It is a very powerful approximation with different applications such as sensing [14–16], bioelectrocatalysis [17,18], corrosion [19,20], and energy storage [3]. This approach has allowed to obtain defined structures following specific nanopatterns, such as linear stripes, controlled by spacer units [21], or almost circles modified with electrografted molecules using a mask [22], and even the formation of islands following a well-defined nanopattern [23]. The surface-generated nanopatterning is intimately related to the composition of the electrografted molecules because they can organize themselves on the surface through self-assembly before grafting.

Despite surface modification protocols with these characteristics are difficult to obtain and require extreme control, the development of new alternatives based on electrografting is a great deal of interest. In this sense, carbon nanodots have been electrografted on highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG). The resulting modified surface is homogeneously covered with spherical granular structures but randomly distributed [24].

Carbon-based materials have grown in popularity as electrode materials in electrochemical devices due to their chemical durability and environmental responsibility, especially in acidic and basic environments and throughout a broad temperature range [25]. At the same time, it has been observed that boron materials-based electrodes increase the capacitance values [26]. All these evidences together with our previous works experience lead us to develop in this work, the synthesis of a new kind of carbon nanodots doped with boron, synthesized by hydrothermal microwave-assisted method. Furthermore, we have studied a strategy of nanostructuring the electrodes’ surfaces using the new boron doped carbon nanodots via electrografting. We have evidenced that perfectly defined hexagonal nanostructures have been generated on HOPG or screen-printed carbon electrodes, due to the controlled electrografting process of the synthesized carbon nanodots. These new hexagonal structures have been exhaustively studied. Initially, the reported hexagons were not expected, but we have demonstrated in this work the possibility of generating them and to have a fine control of their growth. Furthermore, as proof of concept, the hexagonal nanopatterned electrode has been successfully proven as an electric doubled-layer capacitor.

L-arginine, 3,3′-diamino-N-methyldipropylamine, Boric acid, [Ru(bpy)3]2+, potassium hexacyanoferrate (III), potassium chloride, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate, and monobasic sodium phosphate and dibasic sodium phosphate dihydrate ≥99 % were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich-Merck. Sodium nitrite was supplied by Riedel-de-Haen. 37 % (w/w) hydrochloric acid was purchased from Scharlau. Dialysis membrane tubing cutoff (0.1–0.5) kDa was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories. Water was obtained using Millipore Milli-Q system.

B-CNDs were synthesized using: 86 μL 3,3′-diamino-N-methyldipropylamine, 87 mg l-arginine, 15.4 mg boric acid (H3BO3), and 100 μL Milli-Q water. The mixture was irradiated in microwave equipment (CEM Discover®). The experimental conditions during synthesis were kept 180 s: 235 °C constant temperature and 20 bar maximum pressure. Then, the yellow solid obtained was dissolved in 10 mL of MilliQ water and filtered using a 0.1 μm porous filter. After that the obtained suspension was dialyzed during 7 days in a 0.1–0.5 kDa dialysis membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growing of hexagonal nanostructures on HOPG
ce:section.ce:para: Highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG (10 mm × 10 mm × 2 mm)) foil was provided by Goodfellow. It was exfoliated with adhesive tape to renew the flat surface, and it is connected to a copper wire. Then, the plate is used as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl as a reference electrode and a platinum wire as a counter electrode, all of them immersed into an electrochemical cell with and in 2.0 mL of a solution containing diazotized B-CND. The set-up configuration can be observed in Scheme 1 (A). B-CND diazotation was performed in an ice bath by mixing 1.0 mL of a solution containing B-CND in 0.5 M HCl and 1.0 mL of 14.5 mM NaNO2 for 1 h. Hexagonal nanostructures were grown by electrografting by applying consecutive potential cycles from 0.00 V to −0.80 V at 100 mV/s scan rate. Afterward, the electrode was rinsed with Milli-Q water. The electrochemical cycle numbers studied were 10, 25, 32, 40, 50 and 60.
Reactor microwave, CEM Discover microwave system (Matthews (NC), USA) was employed to synthesize the solution of B-CND.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out in a Cary Eclipse Varian spectrofluorophotometer. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured using a UV1700 series spectrometer (Shimadzu Corp.). The measurements were performed in aqueous solutions using a quartz cell with a 1.0 cm optical path. B-CND suspensions were directly measured.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded from KBr pressed pellets using a Brucker IFS66v spectrometer. For this purpose, B-CND suspension was dried at 70 °C for 24 h, and the solid obtained was mixed with KBr in order to create a pressed pellets.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS was used to measure the size of B-CND. B-CND suspensions were diluted (1/50) in 0.01 M KNO3 for DLS measurements.
Zeta potential equipment used was the Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern Instrument Ltd., Grovewood, Worcestershire, UK) was used to measure the surface charge of B-CND.

Elemental analysis of B-CND and CND was performed using a Perkin-Elmer 2400 CHN elemental analyzer. For this porpoise, B-CND and CND were completely dried in an oven at 50 °C. The analyzer's technology is based on the classical organic analysis Pregl-Dumas technique. It is based on the total oxidation of the sample by means of instantaneous and complete combustion. The combustion products: CO2, H2O, NxOy and SO2, are carried by a carrier gas (Helium) to individual and selective sensors of said products (CO2, H2O and SO2) by infrared cells.

Electrochemical measurements were performed with a Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT128N and PGSTAT30 analyzer controlled by NOVA 2.1 software, respectively. A three-electrode glass cell was employed during electrochemical measurements, using an Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl reference electrode and a platinum wire counter electrode. The electrochemical measurements for electrode characterization were performed in a homemade Teflon cell where the exposed area of the HOPG electrode is controlled (diameter of 4 mm). For EDLC characterization, it was noted that the charge-discharge curves and cyclic voltammetry for capacitance measurements were obtained using a two-electrodes configuration, employing two identical HOPG plates, with the same modification process. Set-up configuration can be observed in Scheme 1(B).
Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were obtained with an Agilent 5500 microscope and Olympus cantilevers (RC800PSA, 200_20 mm) operating in contact mode in the air.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the electrode surface samples was carried out with a Phoibos 150 MCD spectrometer equipped with a hemispherical electron analyzer, and using an Al Ka X-ray source (1486.7 eV) with an aperture of 7 mm×20 mm. The base pressure in the ultra-high vacuum chamber was 2 × 10−9 mbar, and the experiments were carried out a room temperature. A 30 eV pass energy was applied for taking the overview sample, whereas 20 eV pass energy was applied for the analysis of the following core level spectra: O(1 s), C(1 s), N(1 s) and B(1 s). XPS spectra regions were fitted and deconvoluted using the fitt-xps software.
The Raman equipment employed was the confocal Bruker Senterra with a 3–5 cm−1 resolution, a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm, and a 50× lens Olympus MPlan Achromat (numerical aperture = 0.75).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were recorded using FRA 4.9 software (Metrohm Autolab). We applied +0.25 V vs. SCE to the working electrode immersed in 1.0 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 7.4 containing 0.1 M KCl.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) experiments were performed on screen-printed carbon electrodes using a bipotentiostat/galvanostat (±4 V DC potential range, ±40 mA maximum measurable current) combined with a Si-Photodiode integrated in an electrochemiluminescence cell from DropSens-Metrohm using [Ru(bpy)3]2+ 2.0 mM in phosphate buffer 0.1 M, pH=8.0. Screen-printed carbon electrodes were provided by DropSens.

For transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Lacey carbon support film copper grids (400 mesh, Electron Microscopy Sciences) were used. Images were obtained with a JEOL JEM 2100 electron microscope. TEM images were obtained at 200k V filament accelerator voltage.

To obtain stable nanostructured surfaces with special properties, such as improved conductivity, electron transfer, and catalytic activity, boron-rich carbon nanodots (B-CND) have been synthesized and covalently immobilized on carbon surfaces. B-CND were synthesized by hydrothermal method assisted by microwave, using l-arginine, 3,3′-diamino-N-methyldipropylamine, and boric acid as precursors. They have been selected carefully to obtain CND doped with B and also N rich. l-arginine, and 3,3′-diamino-N-methyldipropylamine acts as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively, and boric acid as a boron source. The resulting nanomaterial was characterized by different techniques. The elemental analysis of the synthesized B-CND indicates they have 46.6 ± 0.5 %; hydrogen 9.2 ± 0.1 %; nitrogen 19.3 ± 0.1 % and oxygen and/or boron 24.9 ± 0.7 % (calculated by difference). According to these results, the synthesis used gives rise to carbon nanodots with a high percentage of nitrogen, and for the moment we can assume the presence of boron unless we cannot assure it. CND was also synthesized as a control experiment under the same conditions but in the absence of boric acid. Elemental analysis gives as result: carbon 50.3 hydrogen 9.0 ± 0.1 %; nitrogen 21.9 ± 0.2 %; and oxygen 18.7 ± 0.6 % (calculated by difference).

The UV–Vis spectrum shows an absorption band at 310 nm, related to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC units and the n–π* transition of the CO from the core of the carbon nanodots (Fig. 1 A). A broad emission peak at 360 nm is observed when excited at 310 nm (Fig. 1B). Aqueous solutions of B-CND show a light-yellow color in natural light, and they present blue fluorescence when it is irradiated with a hand-held UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1B).

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of B-CND (Fig. 1C) shows the characteristic OH and NH2 stretching bands as a broad band centered at 3480 cm−1 and the bands observed around 2950 cm−1 derived from the C–H bond stretching vibration. CO stretching vibrations appear at 1715 cm−1. The vibrational CC bands appear as a shoulder of 1715 cm−1 main band at around 1647 cm−1 which confirmed the Sp2-graphitic C atoms. At 1450 cm−1, a band related to C–N stretching bonds is visible. These results suggest that B-CND synthesized contain nitrogen in their structure. These bands agree well with the presence of aromatic amines, which are necessary to generate diazotized compounds in the presence of nitrite and acid medium. Also, it can be found bands associated with bound N-B at 1383 cm−1 (B–N stretching bond) and 801 cm−1 (B–N–B bending) [27]. Which indicates that the incorporation of boron has preference linked to nitrogen during the synthetic procedure.

TEM micrograph (Fig. 1D) shows circular shapes associated with B-CND. The diameter of them varies from 15 to 50 nm approximately. The size distribution, estimated by measuring the average size by DLS (Fig. 1E), indicates a size of 25 ± 4 nm ranging, which agrees well with the TEM image shown above. During TEM analysis some aggregates were detected in some zones of the grid (Figure S1), which could agree with the results showed at DLS analysis (Fig. 1E), indicating the presence of other less common aggregates populations at around 90 ± 30 nm and even of thousands of nm.

In addition, the zeta potential value measured at different pHs (Fig. 1F) suggests the presence of negative charges at the B-CND surface at a pH higher than pH=5.3, most likely in the form of carboxylate groups. The isoelectric pH point of B-CND has been determined as 5.3.

We have previously demonstrated that CND with high nitrogen content, specifically with aromatic primary amines on their surface, can be electrografted on carbon electrodes, being covalent attached to conductive surfaces [15,24]. The best conditions to carry out this method were established based on an optimization process: diazotization reaction time of 1 hour and electrografting process based on 50 potential scans between 0.0 V and −0.8 V.

Based on these previous results, we have studied the electrochemical reduction of diazotized B-CND on HOPG monitored by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 2 A). Cyclic voltammograms show the characteristic irreversible reduction process attributed to the reduction of the diazonium salt and how from the second cycle, as the electrode becomes coated, the signal current intensity decreases [28]. These results confirm that aromatic amines cover the B-CND surface, which allows the electrografting over the HOPG surface. Control experiments with non-diazotized B-CND were performed and revealed the absence of the irreversible reduction process (Fig. 2B). This result confirms our supposition and the success of the electrografting of the diazotized B-CND on HOPG. As a preliminary step, the cyclic voltammetry response of this resulting modified surface in phosphate buffer has been recorded and compared with the bare HOPG electrode. As one would expect, the capacitive current suffers a significant increase when the B-CND are electrografted compared to the flat HOPG response (Figure S2), indicating that the HOPG electrode surface has been modified.

The electrografting cyclic voltammetry of B-CND (Fig. 2A) can be compared to the electrografting cyclic voltammetry of carbon nanodots in the absence of boron (CND) (Fig. 2C), showing a similar reduction intensity, which point out that the hexagonal structures are generated as a consequence of a different composition of the electrografted carbon dots (in the presence of boron) and not as a consequence of a different current intensity generated during the CV experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of hexagonal structures on HOPG
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the contact mode AFM images of the HOPG surface before (Fig. 3A) and after (Fig. 3B) its modification with B-CND via electrografting. After being modified, the surface appears covered, not with circular structures as we saw previously by TEM associated with B-CND, instead with hexagonal structures that have been generated covering most of the surface.

As a control, the same modification was carried out but using CND (synthesized in the absence of boron) instead of B-CND. As can be seen in Fig. 3C, globular and almost spherical nanostructures are observed on HOPG, attributed to CND. No hexagonal nanostructures are detected. The role of the presence of boron in the CND is revealed to be essential for generating hexagon-shaped nanostructures on the surface of HOPG. The topographic profiles corroborate the difference. The profile of Fig. 3B shows that the height of hexagons formed is 1.4 nm, compared with the HOPG modified with CND without boron (Fig. 3C), whereas the height of CND is 4 nm. We can also observe that the hexagons are interconnected between themselves and are not isolated.

To delve into this new nanopattern formed by hexagonal nanostructures generated by electrografting of B-CND on HOPG, a detailed study of the different parameters involved in the electrografting process has been carried out. Specifically, the effect of the number of consecutive reductive voltammetric cycles applied has been studied 10, 25, 32, 40, 50, and 60. The resulting modified HOPG surface is observed by AFM (Fig. 4 ).

After 10 reductive scans, we can observe (Fig. 4A and Figure S3) that the surface is decorated with circular nanostructures. Since it can be distinguished different planes of HOPG, the surface is not fully covered by these nanostructures. Moreover, if we study the image in detail by zoom, the nanostructures are not randomly distributed over the surface, they tend to approach and are organized according to a specific distribution, following a defined nanopattern. However, the hexagons have not been formed yet. The height of the electrografted layer is about 7 nm, where B-CNDs are isolated from each other.

By increasing the number of reductive scans to 25, the same behavior is observed. B-CND tend to approximate (Fig. 4B and Figure S4). Nevertheless, the height of the electrografted layer is about 5 nm. On applying more scans, the height of the layer decreases. This may be because the core of the B-CND, consisting mainly of a sp2 and sp3 type carbon core and shell, respectively, undergoes stacking inside, producing a decrease in the height of the nanomaterial as it is applied a reduction potential.

Applying 32 electrografting reduction scans, the image of AFM shows that B-CND is approaching each other (Fig. 4C and Figure S5). It is even observed that the B-CND share a side of a hypothetical hexagon, which is intuited but has not yet been fully formed. The profile shows a height of about 4 nm, confirming that the height of the electrografted layer decreases with increasing the number of cycles. Moreover, another effect is observed, the B-CNDs are ordered by grouping to form hexagons.

After 40 cycles (Fig. 4D and Figure S6), the AFM image clearly shows hexagons on the HOPG surface. 40 cyclic scans are necessary at least to obtain complete form hexagons. These nanostructures obtained in the form of hexagons cover most of the surface of HOPG. It is important to note that the height of the hexagons obtained is about 1.5 nm. As we expected this value is very similar to that shown previously in Fig. 3B By applying more reductive cycles the height decreases significantly, and individual B-CND can no longer be seen, leading to the formation of hexagons. This is revealed in the AFM image obtained in three dimensions (Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5A, which corresponds to a HOPG surface after being exposed to 32 potential cycles in the presence of diazotized B-CND shows individual B-CND because the number of cycles is not enough to form hexagons, while in Fig. 5B is not possible to distinguish individual B-CND and the hexagons have been formed.

To continue, more experiments were performed employing 50 cycles of electrografting (Figs. 4E and Fig. 6 ). As expected, hexagons are formed and cover all the surface homogenously and the height is about 1.6 nm. Once the hexagon has been formed, the height remains constant despite the number of cycles increasing. It is important to emphasize that the hexagons formed are empty inside. Following the bottom-up strategy, a nanoporous surface has been generated with an approximate height of 1.5 nm. This is of great interest for different types of applications such as the development of devices for energy applications [3] and as an electrochemical platform for (bio)sensing [29].

By further increasing the number of cycles up to 60, it is observed in the AFM image that hexagons completely covered the surface of HOPG (Fig. 4F and Figure S7). Although the number of cycles increases, the B-CNDs are not electrografted on top of each other, generating multilayers. The height of the hexagon formed remains constant and corresponds to a layer.

In order to have a global view of how the heigh profile of hexagonal structures change during their formation, in Fig. 7 it can be observed the height profile of different experiments, applying different number of CV scans. It is evident a reduction of height during hexagonal structures generation in the successive CV scans applied, reaching a minimum height in which it almost stabilized.

To summarize the process of the formation of hexagonal nanostructures, as can be deduced from Fig. 4, initially 6 carbon nanodots organize in the vertex of the hexagon. During the electrografting process they seem to lose mass, reorganizing this material around the walls of the hexagon, as a consequence the height of the vertex decreases. Finally, the hexagons are formed when at least 40 CV scans for the electrografting protocol have been applied, and the hexagons generated keep their heigh at around 1.5 nm and the distance between opposites hexagon wall is around 150 nm.

To go further into the process of obtaining the hexagonal structures, additional experiments have been carried out applying a constant reduction potential for 800 s, which is the equivalent time required for applying 50 cycles. The chosen reduction potentials were −0.6 V (Figure S8) and −0.7 V (Figure S9). At −0.6 V, we can observe that large aggregates are formed on a sublayer of small globular aggregates of B-CND with a height of approximately 7 nm. However, at −0.7 V we can appreciate the hexagonal structures previously obtained by reductive cyclic potentials, with a height of 1.6 nm. This highlights the importance of the reduction potential applied to the formation of hexagons.

Several additional controls were also carried out: B-CND without or with previous treatment with NaNO2 for 1 h were directly deposited on the HOPG by drop casting (Figure S10 and S11), and B-CND without treatment with NaNO2 deposited on the HOPG by drop casting were subjected to 50 reductive potential cycles (Figure S12). The AFM images of the resulting modified surfaces (Figure S10, S11, and S12, respectively) show in all cases the absence of hexagonal structures, instead aggregates of B-CND with different sizes are observed. These results reveal that the optimal condition to generate hexagons on the HOPG from B-CND is to apply 50 voltammetric cycles from 0.0 V to −0.8 V in the B-CND solution previously treated with NaNO2.

To prove if the hexagons can be generated in another type of carbon electrode, the electrografting of diazotized B-CND has been carried out on screen-printed carbon electrodes. The resulting modified surfaces have been observed by FE-SEM, since AFM studies cannot be carried out due to the high roughness that these electrodes present. In this case, the formation of hexagonal structures can be intuited (Figure S13), but not with total certainty, as it occurs on the HOPG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of hexagonal structures on HOPG
ce:section.ce:para: XPS analysis of both untreated B-CND, deposited directly by drop casting, and electrografted B-CND was performed to obtain more information about the chemical composition of the hexagonal nanostructures. Fig. 8 shows the XPS spectra of N(1 s) and B(1 s) in both cases, demonstrating the presence of these two elements in both samples. The best fit of the N 1 s core level shows three contributions at binding energies of. 399.1–399.5 eV, 400.7–401.0 eV, and 402.3–402.4 eV, which are assigned to NH2 nitrogen and imines, NH and nitrides/nitriles, and finally ammonium salts and azides [30]. Although both samples present similar components, the spectral profile shows a remarkable difference due to the related ratio components, which are 65.3 ± 0.4 %, 23.1 ± 0.5 %, and 11.6 ± 0.7 % respectively, for the drop-casting while for the electrografted are 42.5 ± 0.3 %, and 41.6 ± 0.1 % and 15.9 ± 0.8 % respectively. The decrease of the first component and the increase of the second component related to the nitrides functionality is remarkable for the electrografted case. In this case amine groups are diazotized and immediately grafted, delivering N2 during this process, which is consistent with the considerable reduction of first N 1 s component and the increment of the second and third components. In addition, the B 1 s core level peak presents three similar components at 187.3 assigned to boron carbide or boride, a second one at 189.3 eV assigned to nitrides and B-N bonds, and the third one at 191.0 eV assigned to (B-O, B-OH, B-C) [31]. The main difference is the increase of the nitride component for the electrografted sample. The fact that B (increment of 8.1 ± 0.3 %) is not as remarkable in the nitrogen case (increment of 19.2 ± 0.3 %) is because N-B and B-nitrides are overlapping, and the destruction and formation of new bonds are partially included in the same component.

The detailed deconvolution of both elements, nitrogen, and boron, suggests the formation of boron nitride species in the case of the electrografting process, which would be related to the generation of the hexagonal structure.

The difference in intensity is also striking, the B-CND electrografting process favors boron and decreases nitrogen. This result is consistent since in the electrografting process gaseous nitrogen is released [28].

Raman spectroscopy is a common technique to characterize different forms of carbon, and it will allow us to analyze the new surface of HOPG with hexagonal nanostructures. Compared to the bare HOPG spectra, the hexagonal nanostructures modified HOPG (Figure S14) shows an intense band at 500 cm−1 and another less intense at 1230 cm−1 due to the effect of doping with boron the CND. They are associated with two maxima in the phonon density of states [32] and are typical of a highly boron-doped carbon [33], as well as the bands located at 2900 cm−1 that can be assigned to the combination of the D and G bands [34]. For either bare or hexagon-modified HOPG, several common bands also appear, around 1550 cm−1, which is attributed to sp2 carbon and described as G-type graphite disorder, and around 2700 cm−1 associated with a band second order 2D

To supply a deeper understanding of the behavior of the hexagonal structures on HOPG surfaces, we studied the electrochemical properties of these electrodes by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and compared them with bare HOPG. Figure S15 shows a Nyquist plot with a sinusoidal wave using K4[Fe(CN)6] as an electrochemical probe. From these plots, the calculated charge resistance for electron transfer (Ret) was 800 ± 25 Ω for bare HOPG and 240 ± 21 Ω for HOPG with hexagonal nanostructures. The generation of these nanostructures produces a considerable decrease in the Ret up to almost three times. These results demonstrate that the conductivity of this new surface is much higher than the bare HOPG. This behavior is in agreement with the results obtained by other authors about the enhanced electrical conductivity in composites due to the presence of carbon nanomaterials [35], as well as in films of boron-doped diamond [36,37].

Additional experiments were performed by voltammetry cyclic, to examine the conductive properties of the hexagonal nanostructures using K4[Fe(CN)6] as a redox probe. The behavior of the redox probe at the bare HOPG electrode shows (Figure S16) that the profile obtained is those expected for an HOPG surface where the basal planes predominate, characterized by their low conductivity [38,39]. It becomes clear that there is no quasi-reversible signal associated with the oxidation and reduction of Fe2+/3+. However, in the presence of hexagons, the separation between the anodic and cathodic peaks is smaller and upgrades considerably. These results confirm that the growth of hexagonal nanostructures on the surface of HOPG considerably improves the electron transfer and conductivity of HOPG. In addition, the newly developed surfaces present a greater relative surface area due to the formation of hexagonal nanostructures bottom-up.

Based on the results obtained above, we continue the study of properties to evaluate their potential applications. Hence, we started to study the electrochemiluminescent properties of the new platform (Figure S17). Carbon nanomaterials containing functional groups such as amine, and hydroxyl groups can act as co-reactant [40,41]. The electrochemiluminescent response of hexagonal structures modified carbon electrodes in the presence of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ as luminophore compared to bare carbon electrodes show a dramatic increase, since B-CND acts as a co-reactant in electrochemiluminescent process, enhancing the electrochemiluminescent signal. This improvement opens the possibility of different applications as electrochemiluminescent sensing platforms. In addition, the hexagonal structures are empty inside. Surfaces with these characteristics have great promise in energy applications such as EDLC design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hexagonal nanostructures based on B-CND as an electric double-layer capacitor
ce:section.ce:para: We wanted to prove also the potential utility of the hexagonal nanostructures modified HOPG as EDLC. For these studies, the charge-discharge curves (C-D) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) for capacitance measurements were obtained using a two-electrode configuration, using two identical HOPG plates, with the same modification process, in any case.

Typically, the capacitance of the EDLC (Fig. 9 B) is evaluated from the charge/discharge cycles slope (Fig. 9A), using Eq. (1):
(1)
C
T o t a l
=
I
2 ×
(
d V
d t
)
× A
where I is the current applied, V is the potential measured over time (t) and A is the electrode geometric area.

The capacitance of the working electrode (Fig. 9D) was calculated from the corresponding voltammograms (Fig. 9C), using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
C
T o t a l
=
∫
V 1
V 2
I
( V )
d V
2 ×
Δ
V × ν × A
Where CTotal is the specific capacitance exhibited by the working electrode in µF/cm2, ∫I(V)dV is the area under the intensity current function between V2 and V1 potentials in Coulombs (C), ΔV is the potential difference between V2 and V1 in volts (V), ν is the voltammogram scan rate in volts per second (V/s) and A is the electrode geometric area.

Great capacitance results have been obtained using hexagon-modified HOPG electrodes, showing a capacitance of 3132 ± 37 µF/cm2. This result is extremely high if we compare with the HOPG electrode (142 ± 5 µF/cm2) without modification and modify by electrografting of CND (not containing B atoms) (232 ± 7 µF/cm2) when no hexagons are formed.

CV results (Fig. 9C and D) clearly show an increase of specific surface capacitance when B-CND are electrografted over HOPG if we compare with the results obtained for CND (without boron and employed as a control) electrografting modification and bare HOPG. The capacitance increase is one order higher, which points out that the hexagonal nanostructures generate a great increase in electrochemical surface area, improving considerably the energy storage capacity.

In order to compare the good capacitance values obtained with other EDLCs developed using carbon nanodots, Table 1 contains relevant information about EDLCs based on carbon nanodots reported in the literature. Unless some excellent results have been previously reported regarding their capacitance normalized with the electrode area, our capacitance values are competitive with most of them. A key point of our methodology is based on its simplicity, based just on an electrochemical method that quickly modifies the HOPG electrode surface, and the low amount of carbon nanodots suspension required to modify a great number of electrodes (just a few millilitres). Furthermore, a strict control of the surface modification using our developed methodology can be applied to a wide range of new doped carbon dots with other interesting heteroátomos and/or metals which can efficiently contribute to increasing the capacitance of new developments.

A bottom-up approach has been developed for the formation of hexagon nanostructures on HOPG. Carbon nanodots doped with different heteroatoms such as boron and nitrogen (B-CND) lead to nanostructured grafted HOPG surfaces. The method selected for their synthesis has been hydrothermal assisted by microwaves from carefully selected precursors. Hexagons-shaped nanostructures covalently attached to the carbon surface are formed. The presence of boron atoms in the B-CND is essential for the formation of the hexagonal structures, and it is controlled by the number of potential scan cycles applied. We demonstrate that the nanopatterns based on hexagonal nanostructured HOPG surfaces can be used as EDLCs among other potential applications. This new nanopattern may offer an alternative way to nanostructure surfaces without the need for large and expensive laboratory facilities, although additional studies will be needed such as density functional theoretical calculations, that will allow us to advance the suitability of the method and the mechanism of nano-hexagons generation.

This work has been supported by the Comunidad Autónoma de Madrid (2021–5A/BIO-20,943 and SI3/PJI/2021–00,341) and the Spanish Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovación (PID2020–116728RB-I00, TED2021–129738B-I00, RED2022–134,120-T and PID2022–142262OA-I00).

Cristina Gutiérrez-Sánchez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Emiliano Martínez-Periñán: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Eva Mateo-Martí: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Encarnación Lorenzo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Santos Gálvez-Martínez for his help with XPS measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2025.106425.
